caveats: a: The BLS occupation population is broader than farm supplies.
a: The software page demonstrates automation scope and customer anecdotes, not representative causal labor savings.
a: Exposure is lower for bulk handling, driving, application support and trusted agronomic advice.
rho: No representative farm-supply-wholesaler implementation-rate study was found.
rho: Small rural firms may adopt more slowly than software reference customers.
rho: Regulated product decisions require human review even when records and recommendations are automated.
e: The Census instrument lists operation types but does not report their shares within the six-digit industry.
e: Cooperatives can provide highly transferable operations yet require a different governance path from ordinary company acquisitions.
e: The frozen LMM count is a margin-bridged estimate.
s5: A single disclosed cooperative acquisition confirms feasibility but not a population transfer rate.
s5: Public deal evidence overrepresents larger cooperative and strategic transactions.
s5: Some cooperative combinations may have governance characteristics unlike a conventional control acquisition.
q: FBN demonstrates price transparency and online comparison but does not measure pass-through from AI savings.
q: Retention differs greatly between commodity fertilizer or feed and differentiated seed, chemicals or technical service.
q: Volatile product costs can obscure operating-efficiency retention in accounting data.
d5: Planted acreage is not an input-volume forecast and excludes livestock, horticulture and smaller crops.
d5: Nominal production expenses are not used as a direct constant-price quantity measure.
d5: Weather and commodity-price cycles can produce large annual departures from the baseline.
o: Restricted-use pesticide requirements do not apply to feed, seed, fertilizer and all chemicals.
o: A national marketplace remains an operator and logistics provider even when it eliminates a local intermediary.
o: The relevant loss is quantity no longer requiring an operator of the lens's kind, not labor automation within a surviving operator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring and seasonal B2B resale of consumable agricultural inputs, usually earning a product spread and sometimes service, delivery, financing, application, or agronomy-related revenue; demand is tied to planted acreage, livestock production, input intensity, weather, and farm economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Farm machinery and equipment dealers, grain elevators and raw-farm-product assemblers, pet-supply wholesalers, pure commission brokers or electronic marketplaces without merchant-wholesale operations, captive manufacturer branches, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers repeatedly supplying farms, ranches, agricultural retailers, or other wholesalers with animal feed, seed, fertilizer, agricultural chemicals, hay, straw, bulbs, and related consumable farm supplies, together with procurement, inventory, delivery, credit, compliance, and product-support workflows.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all NAICS 424 nondurable wholesalers rather than six-digit farm-supply wholesalers, and farm-supply software describes automated workflows rather than wage-weighted task substitution. I map exposure in purchasing, inventory, order entry, replenishment, quoting, accounting, customer support and sales preparation onto the broad occupation evidence, while treating warehouse handling, bulk delivery, field service, agronomic judgment and relationship selling as substantially less exposed. Farm supply operations may be more technical, seasonal and physical than NAICS 424 overall, so the broad proxy likely biases exposure upward.
caveats: The BLS occupation population is broader than farm supplies.
The software page demonstrates automation scope and customer anecdotes, not representative causal labor savings.
Exposure is lower for bulk handling, driving, application support and trusted agronomic advice.
a.change_evidence: A current six-digit OEWS wage-and-employment table for NAICS 424910, paired with task diaries and payroll-verified before/after labor hours from independent farm-supply distributors deploying AI-enabled ERP, replenishment and sales tools.
a.rationale: BLS identifies sales representatives, truck drivers, freight movers and shipping clerks as common nondurable-wholesaler roles. Farm-supply software already automates stock tracking, reorder triggers, purchase orders, order processing, pick-pack and accounting synchronization, but much of the operating labor remains physical or exception-heavy and product advice can carry agronomic and safety consequences.

d5.bridge: USDA projects planted acreage for eight major field crops, not total constant-quality quantity of all feed, seed, fertilizer, chemical and other products sold by NAICS 424910 firms. I use its decline from 247.6 million acres in 2026 to below 246 million from 2028 and toward 241.6 million by 2035 as a physical-demand anchor, with a near-flat five-year base after allowing for livestock/feed demand, input intensity and horticultural supplies. Precision input reduction and farm/customer consolidation could make distributor quantity decline faster than acreage, while weather, biological products or livestock growth could make it rise, so direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: Planted acreage is not an input-volume forecast and excludes livestock, horticulture and smaller crops.
Nominal production expenses are not used as a direct constant-price quantity measure.
Weather and commodity-price cycles can produce large annual departures from the baseline.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year USDA or private forecasts of physical tons, treated acres and seed units by input category, linked to channel shares for independent wholesalers and adjusted for precision-application intensity and livestock inventories.
d5.rationale: Agricultural production requires replenished feed, seed, nutrients and crop protection even in a weak farm-income cycle. USDA's long-term baseline implies only a modest decline in major-crop planted acreage and rising nominal total production expenses after 2026, favoring resilient but not clearly growing constant-quality input quantity over five years.

caveats: The Census instrument lists operation types but does not report their shares within the six-digit industry.
Cooperatives can provide highly transferable operations yet require a different governance path from ordinary company acquisitions.
The frozen LMM count is a margin-bridged estimate.
e.change_evidence: A stratified establishment census matching Census operation type to customer mix, recurring service activity, cooperative or manufacturer ownership, related-party status and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: Most operating merchant wholesalers serve external agricultural customers repeatedly with consumable inputs, inventory and logistics, fitting the lens. The eligible share is reduced for manufacturer branches, thin importers, brokerage or electronic-market models, mixed retail operations, cooperatives whose control cannot be acquired conventionally, related-party entities, and firms misclassified into the target EBITDA band by the frozen margin bridge.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424910 ($9,908,769,099) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424910 ($206,057,886,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,944,016 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424910); class <5: 2,539 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,101,142 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $238,900 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 824 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,959,117 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $791,252 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 593 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,385,032 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,749,278 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 630 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,125,370 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,040,355 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 233 firms, avg revenue/firm $109,882,506 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,493,641 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,457,400,506 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $165,706,438 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,223

o.bridge: EPA inspection rules establish accountable dealer and channel-of-trade obligations for pesticides, while FBN documents online ordering and direct-to-farm delivery for chemicals, seed, feed and animal health products. Neither source measures the share of all year-five farm-supply quantity requiring an LMM merchant wholesaler. I treat hazardous or regulated product custody, inventory, delivery, credit and local exception handling as operator-required, then deduct for manufacturer-direct sales, national online platforms, customer self-service and cooperative or large-chain consolidation. Because only part of the product basket is pesticide-regulated, EPA evidence biases operator necessity upward if generalized to the whole industry.
caveats: Restricted-use pesticide requirements do not apply to feed, seed, fertilizer and all chemicals.
A national marketplace remains an operator and logistics provider even when it eliminates a local intermediary.
The relevant loss is quantity no longer requiring an operator of the lens's kind, not labor automation within a surviving operator.
o.change_evidence: Category-level shipment shares through local independent wholesalers, cooperatives, manufacturer-direct programs and online direct-to-farm platforms, plus customer interviews on agronomy, credit, emergency delivery and compliance needs.
o.rationale: Software can shift ordering and advice to self-service, and direct-to-farm marketplaces show that local incumbents can be bypassed. Still, physical products must be procured, stored and delivered, credit and seasonal exceptions must be managed, and restricted-use pesticide dealers face recordkeeping and certified-customer controls; much surviving quantity therefore needs an accountable operator even if not today's local firm.

caveats: FBN demonstrates price transparency and online comparison but does not measure pass-through from AI savings.
Retention differs greatly between commodity fertilizer or feed and differentiated seed, chemicals or technical service.
Volatile product costs can obscure operating-efficiency retention in accounting data.
q.change_evidence: Customer and supplier contracts, bid histories and invoice-level gross-margin data over renewal cycles, segmented by feed, seed, fertilizer and chemicals, plus documented margin bridges after automation.
q.rationale: Product-spread revenue allows some initial retention because distributor labor is not explicitly reimbursed, but many farm inputs are comparable, high-ticket products bought by cost-sensitive customers. Online price comparisons and direct-to-farm offers reduce information asymmetry, while manufacturer bargaining, seasonal bids and customer negotiations can share automation savings over five years.

caveats: No representative farm-supply-wholesaler implementation-rate study was found.
Small rural firms may adopt more slowly than software reference customers.
Regulated product decisions require human review even when records and recommendations are automated.
rho.change_evidence: A representative five-year implementation cohort reporting workflow activation, integration time, user adoption, exception and override rates, implementation cost, safety incidents and payroll-verified hours saved by function.
rho.rationale: Core inventory, order, purchasing, barcode, invoicing and accounting workflows are commercially digitizable, supporting implementation of roughly half the exposed opportunity within five years. Seasonal demand spikes, inconsistent product and customer data, rural connectivity, hazardous-material and pesticide compliance, legacy systems, field exceptions and change capacity at small operators limit realization.

caveats: A single disclosed cooperative acquisition confirms feasibility but not a population transfer rate.
Public deal evidence overrepresents larger cooperative and strategic transactions.
Some cooperative combinations may have governance characteristics unlike a conventional control acquisition.
s5.change_evidence: A complete five-year census of farm-supply distributor and ag-retail control transfers divided by the active independent-firm base, alongside owner-age, board succession, cooperative member-vote and failed-process data.
s5.rationale: A mature local and cooperative network should generate recurring succession and consolidation events, and CHS's completed acquisition of West Central Ag Services shows that an operating cooperative with locations, employees, grain and agronomy services can transfer. The five-year probability remains below one quarter because member votes, board governance, local relationships, working-capital needs and strategic buyer concentration can impede or reshape qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Essential replenishment demand and repetitive inventory, order and purchasing workflows make targeted automation feasible across a broad operating-firm base.
principal_weakness: Low labor intensity and growing direct-to-farm price transparency limit the size and durability of retained AI-driven economics.

sources.supports: The Census instrument identifies farm supplies sold to farmers or resellers, including feed, seeds, fertilizers and agricultural chemicals, and distinguishes merchant wholesalers from manufacturer branches, agents, brokers and electronic markets, supporting lens boundaries and eligibility caveats.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Wholesale Trade Survey: NAICS 424910

sources.supports: BLS reports common broad-wholesaler occupations including sales representatives, heavy truck drivers, freight movers and shipping and traffic clerks, anchoring the occupation-mix proxy for AI exposure.
sources.title: Merchant Wholesalers, Nondurable Goods: NAICS 424

sources.supports: The fetched page documents farm-supply inventory tracking, barcode scanning, automated reorder triggers and purchase orders, order processing, pick-pack, mobile workflows and accounting integration; a customer quote reports order-processing and sales-integration time improving by 2x.
sources.title: Inventory app for farm machinery and supply distributors

sources.supports: CHS states that it closed the acquisition of West Central Ag Services on January 2, 2025, kept locations operational, continued grain and agronomy services, and offered employment to all employees, supporting control-transfer feasibility.
sources.title: CHS acquires West Central Ag Services to expand reach

sources.supports: USDA projects eight-major-crop planted acreage at 247.6 million acres in 2026/27, below 246 million from 2028 through 2035 and 241.6 million in 2035; it also projects total farm production expenses rising from $465.9 billion in 2026 to $500.7 billion in 2031.
sources.title: USDA Agricultural Projections to 2035

sources.supports: FBN describes online shopping for chemicals, seeds, animal health and feed with direct-to-farm delivery, product comparisons, invoice-based price transparency and online purchasing, supporting channel-bypass and pricing-pressure evidence.
sources.title: How do I get started shopping for inputs on FBN Direct?

sources.supports: EPA states that pesticide marketplace inspections cover correct channel distribution, storage and disposal, and that restricted-use pesticide dealer inspections enforce sales and distribution records and sales only to appropriately certified parties, supporting accountable-operator requirements for part of the basket.
sources.title: Inspections under the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide and Rodenticide Act

title: Farm Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
